interest: Selective-track defenders (conservative value-guardians) protecting high-achieving pupils and non-state maintainer autonomy, alongside equal-opportunity advocates who want intake reform to actually reduce segregation rather than reshuffle it.
value: Excellence/meritocracy and subsidiarity in tension with equity, plus a sub-tension between procedural admission fairness and substantive desegregation outcomes.
fear: That an unvalidated, gameable SES proxy edges out genuinely able pupils and coerces autonomous maintainers, while the reform's proponents fear a cosmetic fix that leaves the underlying stratified queue intact.
assumption: That SES weighting is a poor, manipulable stand-in for disadvantage AND that admission-side changes without binding intake/desegregation targets cannot by themselves shift the composition of who ends up where.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested: the validity and gameability of the SES proxy and whether admission reform alone shortens versus re-labels the queue are open empirical questions the scenario itself flags as unvalidated.
